Application For Order To Show Cause Regarding Contempt
Off Calendar Jul-20-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

REAL PARTY IN INTEREST ACP RE, LTD., ACP RE HOLDINGS, LLC, CASTLEPOINT BERMUDA HOLDINGS, LTD., CASTLEPOINT MANAGEMENT CORP., INTEGON NATIONAL INSURANCE COMPANY, NATIONAL GENERAL HOLDINGS CORP., PRESERVER GROUP, INC., TECHNOLOGY INSURANCE COMPANY, INC., TOWER GROUP, INC., TOWER GROUP INTERNATIONAL, LTD., WILLIAM F. DOVE, WILLIAM FOX, J.R., WILLIAM HITSELBERGER, MICHAEL LEE, HERBERT LEMMER, ELLIOT OROL, WILLIAM ROBBIE, JAMES ROBERTS, STEVEN SCHUSTER, ROBERT SMITH, JAN VAN GORDER, AUSTIN YOUNG, III, MEGHAN ZEIGLER, GEORGE KARFUNKEL, LEAH KARFUNKEL, ESTATE OF MICHAEL KARFUNKEL, BARRY ZYSKIND, MICHAEL KARFUNKEL FAMILY 2005 TRUST, MICHAEL KARFUNKEL 2005 GRANTOR RETAINED, AMTRUST FINANCIAL SERVICES, INC.'s Application For Order To Show Cause Regarding Contempt:

New York Defendants' application for an order to show cause re contempt is denied. By its May 16, 2019 order, the Court acceded to the parties' request that it "clarify" the applicability to the New York Action of the releases and injunctions in the CastlePoint Plan Approval Order and Liquidation Order. It did not, however, enjoin the TruPS Holders from filing an amended complaint-or doing anything else-in the New York Action. Nor did this Court at any time intend or undertake to interfere with the New York court's jurisdiction over or management of that Action. To the contrary, the Court was clear that it had no intention of doing so. The Court understands that the New York Defendants' motion to dismiss the claims asserted in the amended complaint has been fully briefed, and is pending before the New York court. It would be entirely inappropriate for this Court to interfere with the New York court's ruling on that motion or with the parties' conduct of that litigation. Even where there is pending litigation in California between the parties involving the same dispute and implicating a strong state policy, "enjoining proceedings in another state requires an exceptional circumstance that outweighs the threat to judicial restraint and comity principles." (Advanced Bionics Corp. v. Medtronic, Inc. (2002) 29 Cal.4th 697, 708; see also TSMC North America v. Semiconductor Mfg. Internat. Corp. (2008) 161 Cal.App.4th 581, 590-602 [affirming denial of motion for antisuit injunction; no "exceptional circumstances" found].) The parties are admonished not to make any further such requests, or to compound the unnecessary burden on the Court by pursuing needless sanctions motions.

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

Notice Of Motion For Reconsideration Of This Courts May 16, 2019 Order
Off Calendar Aug-13-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
David H. Wollmuth, Esq. of Wollmuth Maher & Deutsch LLP, T:212.382.3300 for moving party; Paul D. Murphy, Esq. of Murphy Rosen LLP, T:310.899.3300, for moving party;
Laurie Hepler, Esq. of Greines, Martin Stein & Richland LLP, T:415.315.1774 for moving party;
Carl Micarelli, Esq. of Debevoise & Plimpton, T:212.909.6813, for interested party ACP, etal;
Maytak Chin, Esq. of Reed Smith LLP, T:415.659.5937, for interested party AMTRUST etal;
Cynthia Larsen, Esq. of Orrick Herrington & Sutcliffe LLP, T:916.329.7970, for the Insurance Commissioner.

RULING: The matter was argued. After arguments, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on ALESCO PREFERRED FUNDING VIII, LTD., ALESCO PREFERRED FUNDING XI, LTD., ALESCO PREFERRED FUNDING XII, LTD., ALESCO PREFERRED FUNDING XIV, LTD., HILDENE OPPORTUNTIES MASTER FUND II, LTD., NFC PARTNERS, LLC, WOLF RIVER OPPORTUNITY FUND LLC, WOLF RIVER PARTNER FUND, AND WT HOLDINGS, INC.'s Motion For Reconsideration Of This Courts May 16, 2019 Order as follows:

Movants' motion for reconsideration of the Court's May 16, 2019 order is denied. Movants have not shown any "new or different facts, circumstances, or law" warranting reconsideration of the Court's order. (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 1008(a).) Nor is the Court inclined to grant reconsideration of the order on its own motion. (See Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal.4th 249.)

The Court made it clear at the very outset of the hearing that it was inclined to give "substantial weight" (but not dispositive weight) to the Insurance Commissioner's views. (RT (Mar. 11, 2019) at 3:25-4:8.) Movants appeared to agree, since in their moving papers they asserted (overly optimistically, as it turned out) that the Commissioner supported their position. (See Mot. at 1:19-21 ["Movants understand that the Insurance Commissioner agrees with their view."].) In any event, Movants had ample opportunity, both at the hearing and in their post-hearing submission, to take issue with the Court's views on that subject. Further, while it is true that the Commissioner changed his position to some degree following the hearing, Movants consented to the procedure proposed by the Court for the parties to submit simultaneous post-hearing briefs and proposed orders. If Movants were taken by surprise by the Commissioner's position, they could have sought leave to file a further brief before the Court issued its order more than 60 days later. Finally, Movants devote the bulk of their motion not to the question whether the Commissioner's position is entitled to deference, but rather to rearguing issues and authority that were raised in the prior briefing, hearing, and post-hearing submissions, or could have been. As such, reconsideration is not appropriate. (See New York Times Co. v. Superior Court (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 206, 212 [moving party must provide satisfactory explanation for the failure to make the showing of new or different facts, circumstances, or law at or before the time the challenged order was issued]; Gilberd v. AC Transit (1995) 32 Cal.App.4th 1494, 1500 [reconsideration not warranted based on claim that trial court misinterpreted applicable law in its initial decision].)

Order signed in open court.
